english: dzin ('dzin), राहुला Rāhula 1. 'seized by Rāhu' (Fouc. Gyatch. II, LVII), obscured, eclipse of the sun or moon, 2. 'catcher of Rāhu,' acc. to the Tibetan legend an epithet given to the deity p'yag-rdór (phyag rdor), acc. to Indian mythology, to Vishnu, who in Tibetan is called kyab-`júg (khyab 'jug) (also kyab-`jug-gsód (khyab 'jug gsod) Cs.); sometimes, however, he is identified with Rāhu himself, for the names gza-sgra-gcan (gza' sgra gcan), gza-sgra-gcan-dzin (gza' sgra gcan 'dzin), gza-kyab-`júg (gza' khyab 'jug), gza-ra-hu-la (gza' ra hu la), and even gza-du-ba-`jug-riń (gza' du ba 'jug ring) (comet!) are used promiscuously. — 3. a son and disciple of Shakyamuni, who received this name on account of an eclipse of the moon taking place at his birth, v. Fouc. Gyatch. II, 389.
Cs.: pf. bsgrańs (bsgrangs), fut. bsgrań (bsgrang), imp. sgroń (sgrong), 1. to enumerate, to reckon up separately. — 2. to upbraid, to reproach.
to cut into small pieces, viz. the picture of an enemy whom one wishes to destroy.

pf. bsgrigs (bsgrigs), fut. bsgrig (bsgrig), imp. sgrig(s) (sgrig(s)), W. *rig-cè* (*rig ce*), to lay or put in order, to arrange, adjust, pań-léb (pang leb) boards or planks, so-pág (so phag) bricks or tiles Glr., kar-yól (dkar yol) plates and dishes, = to lay the cloth; gdan (gdan) stuffed seats or chairs Dzl.; to put or fit together, to join the separate parts of an animal body Glr.; to put close together, side by side, hence W.: *žiń de ñyis rig-te yod* (*zhing de nyis rig te yod*) these two fields are adjacent, *ta dań rig-te yin* (*ta dang rig te yin*) it is situated close to the border; to compile, to write books Glr. — *rig-mo* (*rig mo*) W. tight, close, e.g. a joint, commissure, seam.
Zam.: = mkás-pa (mkhas pa), prudent, skilful, clever, blo sgrin-pa (blo sgrin pa) a penetrating mind Sch.

1. vb. pf. bsgribs (bsgribs), fut. bsgrib (bsgrib), imp. sgrib(s) (sgrib(s)), W. *rib-cè* (*rib ce*), to deprive of light, to darken, to obscure, W. *rib ma rib* (*rib ma rib*) get out of my light! nyi-mai _od-zér bsgribs-nas (nyi ma'i 'od zer bsgribs nas) the light of the sun being obscured, by clouds Glr., by a curtain Zam. — 2. sbst. the state of being darkened, obscuration, gen. fig., mental darkness, sin, also sgrib (sgrib); sèms-can tams-cád-kyi sgrib-pa sél-ba (sems can thams cad kyi sgrib pa sel ba) frq., hence sgrib-pa-rnam-sél (sgrib pa rnam sel) n. of a Boddhisatva; sgrib-pa lńa (sgrib pa lnga) Dzl., the five obscurations caused by sin, prob. = पञ्चकषाय Burn. II, 360. — 3. adj darkened, obscured, dark; sinner, bdag-rań sgrib-pa čé-am am (bdag rang sgrib pa che am am) I so great a sinner? Pth. — *dib-ma* (*dib ma*) C., *rib-ma* (*rib ma*) W. shelter, fence, e.g. at the side of a field against the wind.

pf. bsgrims (bsgrims), fut. bsgrim (bsgrim), imp. sgrim(s) (sgrim(s)), Cs.: 'to hold fast, to force or twist together; to endeavour'; Sch. also: 'to squeeze in, cram in; to be overhasty, confused'. Only the following phrases came to my notice: *kú'-pa ḍim-pa* (*ku' pa dim pa*) C. to twist or twine a thread; *rig-pa ḍim* (*rig pa dim*) C. take care! (collect your thoughts!); *ḍim-tog-čan* (*dim tog can*) Sp. inquisitive, curious. Some passages in B., e.g. blo-bsgrims (blo bsgrims) (explained by blo-`dás (blo 'das) Zam.) are as yet dubious as to their sense.

pf. and fut. bsgril (bsgril), W. *(s)ril-cè* (*(s)ril ce*), (cf. gril-ba (gril ba) 1. and _kril-ba ('khril ba)), 1. to wind or wrap round e.g. a bit of cloth round one's finger; to roll, wrap, or wind up; ril-bur (ril bur) to roll or form into a pill Med.; to make fast or tight, lhód-pa (lhod pa) what is loose; p'yogs gcig-tu sgril-ba (phyogs gcig tu sgril ba) to gather into a heap, to heap or pile up, to sweep together; hence sgril-bas (sgril bas) (also dril-bas (dril bas) Glr.) to sum up all, taking all together, in short Lt.; mjúg-ma sgril-ba (mjug ma sgril ba) to wag the tail, mi-la (mi la) at a person (of dogs) Mil.; to roll, e.g. a large stone to some place. — 2. to multiply Wdk., frq.; bsgril-ma (bsgril ma) a doubled and twisted thread or cord Sch.; sgril-šiń (sgril shing) a wooden roll, round which paper etc. is wound; the rolling-pin of bakers. — sgril-šóg (sgril shog), W. *šog-ril* (*shog ril*), rolled paper Cs.
pf. bsgrugs (bsgrugs), fut. bsgrug (bsgrug), imp. sgrug(s) (sgrug(s)), W. *rúg-cè(s)* (*rug ce(s)*), to collect, gather, pluck, pick up e.g. wood, flowers, vermin etc.

Ld. *sruńs* (*srungs*), C. *ḍum* (*dum*), fable, legend, tale (to the uncultivated mind of the Tibetan, destitute of any physical and historical knowledge of the countries and people beyond the boundaries of his native soil, the difference
